challenges.prompt: The page below loads in a browser. Where does the text 'My First Page' actually appear?
options: In the browser tab (and search results) — not as visible text on the page. The page shows only 'Hello, World!'.
As a large heading at the top of the page, above 'Hello, World!'.
Both in the browser tab and as a heading on the page.
Nowhere — a <title> inside <head> is ignored by the browser.
challenges.explanation: The <title> lives in the <head>, which holds metadata rather than visible content, so its text shows only in the browser tab and in search results. To put a visible heading on the page you use an <h1> inside <body> — which is why only 'Hello, World!' is rendered.

challenges.prompt: This page is supposed to use the modern HTML5 standard, but something at the very top is wrong. What's the bug?
challenges.code: Hi, I'm Alex!
options: The <meta charset> tag must come after the <title>, not before it.
The declaration should be <!DOCTYPE html>, not <!DOCTYPE html5> — there is no version number.
<html lang="en"> is invalid; the lang attribute is not allowed on the root element.
The <h1> should be inside <head> so the browser can find the page's main topic.
challenges.explanation: The modern declaration is simply <!DOCTYPE html> with no version number — that single form tells the browser to use the HTML5 rulebook. Writing <!DOCTYPE html5> is not valid and can drop the browser into quirky legacy rendering mode.

challenges.prompt: Complete the two essential <head> meta tags: one that sets the character encoding, and one whose name makes the page mobile-friendly.
challenges.codeTemplate:
<head>
  <meta charset="{{blank}}" />
  <meta name="{{blank}}" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" />
  <title>My First Page</title>
</head>
challenges.explanation: charset="UTF-8" tells the browser how to decode the bytes in your file so accented letters, symbols, and emoji render correctly. The <meta name="viewport"> tag stops mobile browsers from shrinking the page, making it the entry point to a mobile-friendly site.

challenges.prompt: Arrange these lines into a valid HTML5 boilerplate document, correctly nested from the outside in.
challenges.explanation: Every document opens with the <!DOCTYPE html> declaration, then the <html> root wraps everything. Inside, <head> (metadata like <title>) comes before <body> (the visible content), and each element closes in the reverse order it opened. Consistent indentation makes this nesting tree visible at a glance.
